title: The late Marx's revolutionary roads: colonialism, gender, and indigenous communism [Book Review]
author: Wapeemukwa

opening_move.description: The review opens by situating Anderson's book as recovering Marx's neglected post-1868 writings, arguing against scholars who dismiss this period and establishing its significance for understanding Marx's revised theory of revolution.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Marx's final years show a fundamental revision of his theory of revolution, race, and gender — recovering this corrects an Eurocentric and teleological reading of Marxism

rivals_engaged: Stedman-Jones and others who dismiss late Marx
theoretical_positioning.description: The review endorses Anderson's recovery of late Marx while pressing an immanent critique: Anderson's use of 'dialectic' risks abstracting Haitian revolutionary history into a theoretical model.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: textual and intellectual history; recovery of Marx's late notebooks
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review; primarily expository with a focused immanent critique about the abstraction of Haiti from its own intellectual-political history.

confidence_notes: Book review genre. The immanent critique about abstracting Haiti into a theoretical model is the reviewer's substantive contribution.
